content:
# Genesis Chapter 8 — The SOPHIA Consensus Bible
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA (Σοφία — Wisdom) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 5r–5v | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_008_page_001.jpg, GEN_008_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0009
**Constituent Translations:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

**8:1** But God remembered Noah — and every living thing, and all the animals that were with him in the ark. And God caused a wind to pass over the earth, and the waters subsided.

**8:2** The fountains of the deep and the windows of the heavens were closed, and the rain from the heavens was restrained.

**8:3** And the waters receded from upon the earth, going and receding. And at the end of one hundred and fifty days the waters had diminished.

**8:4** And the ark came to rest in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, upon the mountains of Ararat.

**8:5** And the waters continued to diminish until the tenth month. In the tenth month, on the first day of the month, the tops of the mountains appeared.

**8:6** And it came to pass at the end of forty days that Noah opened the window of the ark that he had made.

**8:7** And he sent out the raven, and it went out going and returning until the waters were dried up from upon the earth.

**8:8** And he sent out the dove from beside him, to see whether the waters had subsided from the face of the ground.

**8:9** But the dove found no resting place for the sole of her foot, and she returned to him into the ark, for the waters were upon the face of all the earth. And he stretched out his hand and took her and brought her in to him into the ark.

**8:10** And he waited still another seven days, and again he sent out the dove from the ark.

**8:11** And the dove came to him toward evening — and behold, a freshly plucked olive leaf was in her mouth. And Noah knew that the waters had subsided from upon the earth.

**8:12** And he waited still another seven days, and sent out the dove; and she did not return to him again.

**8:13** And it came to pass in the six-hundred-and-first year, in the first month, on the first day of the month, the waters had dried up from upon the earth. And Noah removed the covering of the ark and looked, and behold — the face of the ground was dry.

**8:14** And in the second month, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, the earth was dry.

**8:15** And God spoke to Noah, saying:

**8:16** "Go out from the ark — you, and your wife, and your sons, and your sons' wives with you.

**8:17** Every living thing that is with you of all flesh — the birds and the beasts and every creeping thing that creeps upon the earth — bring out with you, that they may swarm upon the earth and be fruitful and multiply upon the earth."

**8:18** And Noah went out, and his sons and his wife and his sons' wives with him.

**8:19** Every beast, every creeping thing, and every bird — everything that moves upon the earth — went out from the ark by their families.

**8:20** And Noah built an altar to the LORD, and took from every clean animal and from every clean bird and offered burnt offerings upon the altar.

**8:21** And the LORD smelled the pleasing aroma, and the LORD said in his heart: "I will not again curse the ground on account of the human being, for the inclination of the human heart is evil from his youth; and I will not again strike down every living thing as I have done.

**8:22** While the earth remains — seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night — shall not cease."

---

## SOPHIA's Arbitration Notes

### Manuscript Verification

Both folios of the Leningrad Codex (B19A) were examined directly. Folio 5r (GEN_008_page_001.jpg) and Folio 5v (GEN_008_page_002.jpg) are legible in the three-column layout, with full Tiberian vocalization (qamets, patah, tsere, hiriq, shureq) and cantillation marks throughout. The divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) is visible in verses 20–21 with standard Masoretic qere notation; אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) appears in verses 1 and 15. The plural הָרֵי אֲרָרָט in verse 4 is confirmed. The text of verse 22 reads לֹא יִשְׁבֹּתוּ without variant. No divergence from the MT as presented by all three agents was detected from manuscript inspection.

---

### Arbitration Decisions by Verse

---

#### 8:1 — "But God remembered" / Opening Conjunction

**The issue:** EMET opens "And God remembered"; LOGOS opens "And God remembered"; PNEUMA opens "But God remembered." The Hebrew וַיִּזְכֹּר begins with the consecutive vav, which in standard narrative sequence is rendered "and." PNEUMA's "but" reflects the adversative sense — the preceding silence of the 150-day high-water period is now broken by divine action — and was commended by PNEUMA's translator's note as capturing the dramatic reversal. All three agents agree on "God remembered" rather than any weakened paraphrase.

**Decision: "But God remembered."** The adversative "but" is adopted from PNEUMA. The consecutive vav in biblical Hebrew regularly carries an adversative or resumptive sense when it follows a narrative pause or peril, and in context — after the 150 days of silent prevailing waters that ended chapter 7 — the English "but" more accurately conveys what the Hebrew vav performs here than the neutral "and." LOGOS's extensive note on the covenantal character of זָכַר (zakar) is adopted wholesale as the interpretive framework. Divine remembering is not mental recall but covenantal action; the translation "remembered" is retained (against any paraphrase) as the most formally faithful and theologically loaded rendering.

---

#### 8:1 — רוּחַ: "wind" vs. "spirit"

**The issue:** All three agents render רוּחַ as "wind" in the translation text itself. EMET brackets the question "wind/spirit" most explicitly, citing the Gen. 1:2 echo (רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים hovering over the waters) and presenting a table showing the structural parallel. LOGOS similarly elaborates the echo at length. PNEUMA names itself after this word (πνεῦμα) and frames the entire chapter through the lens of the ruach's continuous work from creation through re-creation.

**Decision: "wind."** All three agents converge on this rendering, and the consensus follows. The Hebrew here is רוּחַ without the construct "of God" (unlike Gen. 1:2), and the immediate context — God causing it to "pass over" the flooded earth to dry the waters — is concretely meteorological. The translation renders "wind" as the primary gloss. However, the theological resonance with Gen. 1:2 is fully acknowledged in the notes: the same word that initiated creation now initiates re-creation. No translation note can close the semantic range of רוּחַ; the choice of "wind" is the most transparent to the reader while the larger resonance is carried by the literary context and the notes below.

The phrase "God caused a wind to pass over the earth" is confirmed over PNEUMA's slightly more dynamic "sweep" (which was attractive but not formally supported — עָבַר means "to pass over," not "to sweep").

---

#### 8:3 — הָלוֹךְ וָשׁוֹב: "going and returning" vs. "going and receding"

**The issue:** In verse 3 this gerundive pair describes the waters; in verse 7 the same construction (יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב) describes the raven. EMET renders verse 3 as "going and returning"; LOGOS as "going and receding"; PNEUMA as "receding and withdrawing." The infinitive absolute construction הָלוֹךְ וָשׁוֹב literally means "going and returning" — the same idiom used for the raven in verse 7. The waters ebb and return, ebb and return, progressively receding.

**Decision: "going and receding."** LOGOS's rendering is adopted. For the waters in verse 3, "going and receding" is preferable to the literal "going and returning" because "returning" in English implies the waters are coming back to their flood level, which is the opposite of what the narrative means. The construction expresses rhythmic, progressive retreat — two steps forward, one back, but ever diminishing. "Receding" captures the directional vector of the process. PNEUMA's "receding and withdrawing" is more redundant than illuminating. The note on the infinitive absolute gerundive construction (EMET's analysis) is adopted in full.

---

#### 8:4 — "mountains of Ararat" (plural confirmed)

**The issue:** All three agents agree: הָרֵי אֲרָרָט is plural — "mountains of Ararat," not "Mount Ararat." The LXX Vaticanus (ἐπὶ τὰ ὄρη τὰ Αραράτ) confirms the plural. There is no disagreement.

**Decision: "upon the mountains of Ararat."** Unanimous. The Masoretic plural is preserved against the long-standing popular tradition of "Mount Ararat" (which reflects interpretive topographical tradition, not the Hebrew text). The ark rested somewhere in the Ararat mountain range (ancient Urartu); the text does not name a peak.

---

#### 8:7 — The Raven: "going and returning" vs. "went back and forth"

**The issue:** וַיֵּצֵא יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב — EMET renders "going and returning"; LOGOS renders "it went back and forth"; PNEUMA renders "going and returning." The infinitive absolute יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב is a gerundive pair expressing iterative, continuous motion. The LXX Vaticanus diverges significantly: ὃ ἐξελθὸν οὐχ ὑπέστρεψεν — "which going out did not return" — reading the raven as a failed messenger that simply never comes back. LOGOS notes this LXX variant and explicitly rejects it in favor of the MT's iterative reading, citing how it flattens the raven/dove contrast. EMET and PNEUMA both follow the MT.

**Decision: "going and returning."** The MT's iterative infinitive absolute construction is followed over the LXX. The raven goes out and comes back, goes out and comes back — circling the ark, landing on floating debris, providing no usable intelligence — until the earth dries. This behavior is ecologically coherent for a scavenger (it can feed on carrion over water and has no need to return to the ark for food) and narratively creates the sharp contrast with the dove, which cannot land on water. "Going and returning" is preferred over LOGOS's "went back and forth" because it preserves the Hebrew infinitive absolute word-pair form that mirrors the structure of verse 3 (הָלוֹךְ וָשׁוֹב) and verse 3's waters — the raven's behavior echoes the waters' own going-and-returning motion. The symmetry is intentional.

---

#### 8:9 — "resting place" / manoach

All three agents render "no resting place for the sole of her foot." EMET and LOGOS both note the מָנוֹחַ / נֹחַ wordplay. PNEUMA develops it most fully, noting that there was no *noach* for the dove. The consensus preserves this exact phrasing without modification. The anatomical specificity of כַּף-רַגְלָהּ ("the sole of her foot") is retained; it is not weakened to "a place to land."

---

#### 8:11 — The Olive Leaf: טָרָף (taraf)

**The issue:** EMET renders "freshly plucked olive leaf" and notes that טָרָף means "torn, freshly picked" — not a floating dead leaf but one newly taken from a living tree. LOGOS renders "freshly plucked olive leaf" and provides the same analysis. PNEUMA renders "freshly plucked olive leaf." All three agree. The LXX Vaticanus renders φύλλον ἐλαίας κάρφος ("a twig/small piece of olive"), losing the "freshly torn" nuance. All three agents explicitly reject the LXX here.

**Decision: "a freshly plucked olive leaf."** Unanimous. The word טָרָף is a passive participle from טָרַף ("to tear, pluck") meaning freshly and violently torn from a living branch. The theological force — the leaf is *alive*, torn from a *living* tree, signaling that vegetation has survived the flood — depends on preserving this word. "Freshly plucked" captures it adequately in English. The LXX's "twig" loses the freshness; the consensus follows the MT.

One minor adjustment: PNEUMA renders "in her beak" (anatomically accurate for a dove); EMET and LOGOS render "in her mouth" (literal translation of בְּפִיהָ, peh = mouth). The consensus follows the literal Hebrew: "in her mouth." The Hebrew consistently uses פֶּה for both human mouths and bird beaks; "mouth" is retained.

---

#### 8:13 — The Double Dryness (חָרַב / יָבֵשׁ)

All three agents note the two distinct Hebrew words for dryness: חָרַב (charav — to dry up, appear dry) in verse 13, and יָבֵשׁ (yavesh — fully dry) in verse 14. EMET provides the fullest analysis: חָרַב suggests the visual appearance of dryness, while יָבֵשׁ (the word used for dry land in Gen. 1:9-10) signals structural, habitable dryness. The consensus renders verse 13 "the face of the ground was dry" and verse 14 "the earth was dry," matching the LOGOS formal rendering, which distinguishes the two states without over-explaining them.

---

#### 8:20 — Divine Name: YHWH / "the LORD"

**The issue:** The altar is built לַיהוָה. All three agents render this "to the LORD." The divine name pattern in chapter 8 is: אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) in verses 1 and 15 (cosmic/Priestly material); יְהוָה (YHWH) in verses 20–21 (altar and promise, YHWH-strand material). All three agents preserve this alternation without harmonization.

**Decision:** Confirmed. "God" for אֱלֹהִים; "the LORD" for יְהוָה. No harmonization. The divine name shift at verse 20 is structural and theologically significant: the creation-restoring acts (wind over waters, exit command) belong to Elohim; the personal, covenantal response to Noah's worship belongs to YHWH.

---

#### 8:20–21 — The Noah/reyach nichoach Wordplay

**The issue:** רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ — the "pleasing aroma." All three agents render this phrase identically. EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA all note that נִיחֹחַ (nichoach) is cognate to נֹחַ (Noah) and נוּחַ (to rest) — the "aroma of rest," the aroma that soothes (brings to rest) the divine response. The entire Noah-nuach wordplay thread is analyzed in all three agents: 5:29 (Noah as comfort/rest), 8:4 (the ark rests, נוּחַ), 8:9 (manoach — no resting place for the dove), 8:21 (nichoach — the aroma of rest). No disagreement exists on the translation phrase itself.

**Decision: "the pleasing aroma."** Unanimous. The English cannot fully render נִיחֹחַ's acoustic resonance with נֹחַ; the wordplay is noted and preserved in commentary rather than attempted in the translation text.

---

#### 8:21 — "said in his heart" (divine interior monologue)

**The issue:** וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-לִבּוֹ — EMET renders "YHWH said in his heart" (substituting "the LORD" for the divine name in the translation). LOGOS renders "the LORD said in His heart." PNEUMA renders "the LORD said in his heart." All three agents identify this as one of only two places in the Hebrew Bible where God speaks not to a human but to his own heart — an interior divine resolve. The LXX Vaticanus reads καὶ εἶπεν κύριος ὁ θεὸς διανοηθείς ("and the Lord God thought/reflected"), adding "God" and using a cognitive rather than speech verb — both rejected by all three agents.

**Decision: "and the LORD said in his heart."** Unanimous. The intimate phrasing is preserved exactly. The shift from speech-to-Noah to speech-to-himself is a pivotal narrative event: the reader is granted access to God's private resolve before it is formalized as the covenant of Genesis 9. Capitalizing "His" (LOGOS) vs. lowercase "his" (EMET, PNEUMA): the consensus follows EMET and PNEUMA in rendering without capitalization, consistent with the convention of not adding additional divine elevation through typography beyond the standard rendering of the name.

---

#### 8:21 — The Yetzer Paradox: Same Diagnosis, Opposite Conclusion

**The issue:** כִּי יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם רַע מִנְּעֻרָיו — "for the inclination of the human heart is evil from his youth." This is one of the most profound theological cruxes in the Pentateuch. The same diagnosis that provided the reason for the flood in 6:5 (יֵצֶר לִבּוֹ רַק רַע כָּל הַיּוֹם — "every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all the day") now provides the reason for the promise never to flood again. All three agents recognize and analyze this reversal with remarkable agreement.

The word יֵצֶר (yetzer) requires careful rendering: it derives from יָצַר ("to form, fashion"), the same root as God forming (וַיִּיצֶר) the adam from dust in Gen. 2:7. The יֵצֶר is the "formed tendency" — the dispositional shape the heart has been fashioned with. EMET renders "inclination"; LOGOS renders "inclination"; PNEUMA renders "inclination." The LXX renders it διάνοια ("thought, mind") — all three agents reject this because it loses the specific "formed tendency" concept that is foundational to later Jewish theology (yetzer ha-ra / yetzer ha-tov). All three agents also note the addition of מִנְּעֻרָיו ("from his youth") compared to 6:5 — this specification marks the evil inclination as constitutional, present from youth, not acquired.

**Decision: "the inclination of the human heart is evil from his youth."** Unanimous on all key words. The "human being" / "human heart" rendering: EMET uses "the adam" (preserving the Hebrew's specific wordplay on adam/adamah — the person from the ground, whose evil is a reason not to curse the ground again); LOGOS uses "the human beings" / "the human heart"; PNEUMA uses "the human being" / "the human heart." The consensus adopts "the human being" / "the human heart" — PNEUMA's more natural English rendering — while noting the adam/adamah wordplay in the arbitration notes. Translating ha-adam as "the human being" (singular) rather than "human beings" (plural, LOGOS) follows the Hebrew's singular usage and preserves the connection to the adam of the creation and curse narratives.

The conjunction: EMET renders "for the inclination ... is evil" as a causal reason for mercy. The paragraph structure: PNEUMA formats the divine interior monologue as a set speech with a new line; LOGOS keeps it in continuous prose; EMET keeps continuous prose. The consensus follows LOGOS/EMET in presenting the divine speech as continuous prose within the verse, since the Hebrew does not signal poetic lineation here, and the speech runs directly from "I will not again curse" through verse 22.

---

#### 8:21 — "curse the ground on account of the human being" (בַּעֲבוּר הָאָדָם / אֲדָמָה)

**The issue:** The wordplay between הָאָדָם (ha-adam, the human being) and הָאֲדָמָה (ha-adamah, the ground) is one of the most structurally important puns in Genesis — from the creation of ha-adam from ha-adamah (2:7), to the curse of the adamah on account of the adam (3:17), to the flood as the extension of that curse, to this moment where YHWH vows not to continue cursing the adamah on account of the adam. EMET preserves this by translating "the ground" and "the adam." LOGOS translates "the ground" and "the human beings." PNEUMA translates "the ground" and "the human being."

**Decision:** "I will not again curse the ground on account of the human being." The consensus preserves the singular "the human being" (ha-adam) and "the ground" (ha-adamah), keeping the wordplay present even in English for readers attentive to the creation narrative's consistent use of these terms. The adam/adamah correspondence is the structural spine of the curse-and-uncurse movement across Genesis 2–9.

---

#### 8:22 — The Six Pairs / לֹא יִשְׁבֹּתוּ / shavat irony

**The issue:** The verse lists three agricultural-cosmological cycles plus the daily cycle, expressed as four paired opposites. All three agents agree on the rendering of the pairs themselves: seedtime and harvest / cold and heat / summer and winter / day and night. EMET counts "four pairs"; the structural note is that there are three seasonal/climatic pairs plus one diurnal pair — covering the full range of temporal regularity. All three agents identify the shavat/Shabbat irony: the verb לֹא יִשְׁבֹּתוּ uses the root of שַׁבָּת (Shabbat) — the cycles "shall not Sabbath," shall not cease. The regular working rhythms of creation will never themselves take a rest.

**Decision: "shall not cease."** Unanimous on the translation. EMET's rendering "shall not cease" is preferred over more expansive paraphrases. The framing phrase: EMET renders "While all the days of the earth remain"; LOGOS renders "While the earth remains"; PNEUMA renders "As long as the earth endures." The consensus adopts LOGOS's "While the earth remains" as the most balanced formal-idiomatic rendering of עֹד כָּל-יְמֵי הָאָרֶץ — it is idiomatic English without importing the additional force of PNEUMA's "endures" (which implies precariousness not present in the Hebrew).

The LXX variant in verse 22 (θέρος καὶ ἔαρ — "summer and spring" rather than "summer and winter") is noted but not followed. The MT's קַיִץ וָחֹרֶף (summer and winter) is the correct reading; the LXX's substitution of "spring" for "winter" reflects either a different seasonal frame of reference or a later adaptation and does not represent the superior text.

---

### Structural and Theological Summary

Genesis 8 is the re-creation chapter. Its structure is a precise literary chiasm around the theological pivot of verse 1: "But God remembered Noah." The de-creation of chapter 7 (fountains of the deep burst, windows of heaven opened, waters prevailing) is systematically reversed in verses 1–5 (fountains closed, windows closed, waters subsiding). The narrative moves from cosmic catastrophe through the intimate bird missions to the first altar in Scripture, and closes with the divine interior resolve of verses 21–22 — a private commitment that will be formalized as the Noahic covenant in chapter 9.

**Key theological movements preserved in the consensus:**

1. **Zakar as covenantal action:** "God remembered" is not cognitive recall but the initiation of saving, covenant-loyal intervention. The flood ends because God turns his faithful attention back toward the one who survived.

2. **Ruach as the re-creation signal:** The wind over the waters echoes Gen. 1:2 deliberately. Re-creation employs the same instrument as creation. The flood did not defeat God's creational intent.

3. **The bird missions as progressive revelation:** The raven (going and returning, uninformative) and the dove (three sendings with escalating results) together narrate the return of habitable earth. The dove's three outcomes — nothing, olive leaf, no return — map the transition from flood to new world.

4. **The first altar:** Noah's first act outside the ark is worship. The altar (מִזְבֵּחַ) appears for the first time in the Bible. The burnt offerings (עֹלֹת) are entirely consumed — total consecration.

5. **The Noah/nichoach/manoach thread:** The name נֹחַ (rest), the dove finding no מָנוֹחַ (resting place), and the sacrifice producing a רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ (aroma of rest) form an architectural wordplay across the chapter. The man of rest offers the rest-aroma; the chapter arrives at rest.

6. **The yetzer paradox:** The same diagnosis (the inclination of the human heart is evil) that caused the flood now causes the promise never to flood again. The flood was not a solution to human evil; it demonstrated the futility of destruction as correction. YHWH chooses patient mercy over repetitive judgment.

7. **The shavat irony:** The cycles of creation "shall not shavat" — shall not cease/Sabbath. The regular rhythms of time are the guarantee of cosmic stability. YHWH's faithfulness is inscribed into the regularity of creation itself.

8. **Divine name pattern:** Elohim in cosmic/Priestly material (vv. 1, 15); YHWH in covenantal/personal material (vv. 20–21). No harmonization.

---

### LXX Variants — Consensus Decisions

| Verse | MT | LXX (Vaticanus) | Consensus Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8:1 | וַיַּעֲבֵר רוּחַ ("caused a wind to pass") | ἐπήγαγεν πνεῦμα ("brought a pneuma upon") | MT followed; "pass over" preferred to "bring upon" |
| 8:4 | הָרֵי אֲרָרָט ("mountains of Ararat") | ὄρη τὰ Αραράτ ("mountains of Ararat") | Both agree; plural confirmed |
| 8:7 | יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב ("going and returning") | οὐχ ὑπέστρεψεν ("did not return") | MT followed; LXX flattens the iterative idiom |
| 8:11 | עֲלֵה-זַיִת טָרָף ("freshly plucked olive leaf") | φύλλον ἐλαίας κάρφος ("a twig of olive") | MT followed; LXX loses the freshness/vitality of טָרָף |
| 8:21 | יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם ("inclination of the human heart") | διάνοια τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ("thought/mind of man") | MT followed; יֵצֶר is a specific theological term (formed tendency) |
| 8:21 | אֶל-לִבּוֹ ("to his heart") | ἐν τῇ διανοίᾳ ("in his understanding") | MT followed; "said in his heart" preserves the intimacy of divine interior speech |
| 8:22 | קַיִץ וָחֹרֶף ("summer and winter") | θέρος καὶ ἔαρ ("summer and spring") | MT followed; LXX variant likely reflects different seasonal reference frame |

---

*Signed: SOPHIA (Σοφία — Wisdom), Consensus Arbiter*
*Consensus produced from EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), and PNEUMA (Dynamic) translations.*
*Source: Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 5r–5v. Manuscript images photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (© Bruce E. Zuckerman).*
*LXX consultation: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana.*
